ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for crystal dumplings (wheat starch and tapioca starch skin with chive and shrimp filling). Mapped to the closest parent class 'dumpling food product'.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers steamed dumplings generically; no specific code exists for crystal/translucent wheat-starch dumplings with chive-shrimp filling.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for crystal dumplings or dim sum dumpling category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard dim sum serving of Crystal Dumplings consists of approximately 3 pieces weighing 90 g, typically served in a bamboo steamer basket during yum cha.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Dumplings, steamed, with shrimp and vegetable filling (closest analogous entry); supplemented with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database for dim sum reference values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for crystal dumplings (wheat starch + tapioca starch skin). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central steamed shrimp-and-vegetable dumpling entries and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore dim sum nutrient data. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with these references and have been retained with minor adjustments. Saturated fat estimated at 0.9 g/100 g based on sesame oil and shrimp fat profile. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not available in approved sources for this preparation. Cholesterol estimated at 55 mg/100 g based on shrimp content. Water content set to -1 as moisture data for this specific preparation is not available in approved sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on wheat starch and tapioca starch dumpling skin, steamed preparation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crystal dumplings in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 50 (low) is estimated by analogy with steamed wheat-starch dim sum items (har gow / shrimp dumplings) and mixed starch-based steamed dumplings documented in Atkinson et al. 2021 (GI range 45–55 for steamed wheat-starch dim sum). The fiber from garlic chives (2.1 g/100 g) and protein from shrimp moderate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 90 g serving = (50 × 22.1 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 ≈ 11.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 320 mg/100 g; a 90 g serving delivers approximately 288 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit Crystal Dumplings to one serving (90 g) and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or chili oil, as the combined sodium load can approach 500–600 mg per meal occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (24.5 g/100 g) with low GI (50) and 2.1 g dietary fibre per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Crystal Dumplings have a low glycemic index of approximately 50 and provide 2.1 g dietary fibre per 100 g, making a single 90 g serving (GL ≈ 11) acceptable for most individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal with non-starchy vegetables; portion control is essential to avoid cumulative carbohydrate loading.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.9 g/100 g), steamed preparation, shrimp provides omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed Crystal Dumplings are a heart-friendly dim sum choice due to their low saturated fat content and steaming method that avoids added frying oils; shrimp contributes modest omega-3 fatty acids, though sodium intake from dipping sauces should be monitored by individuals with cardiovascular disease.

shellfish_allergy.guidance: Crystal Dumplings contain shrimp and are contraindicated for individuals with a shellfish allergy; cross-contamination risk is high in dim sum kitchens.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crystal Dumplings, known in Cantonese as 韭菜餃 (Jiǔcài Jiǎo or Gau2 Coi3 Gaau2) and commonly called Chive Dumplings or Jiu Cai Jiao, are a classic Cantonese dim sum item distinguished by their translucent, glass-like skin. The wrapper is made from a blend of wheat starch and tapioca starch, which becomes delicately clear and slightly chewy when steamed, allowing the vibrant green of the garlic chive filling and the pink of the shrimp to show through. The filling combines fresh garlic chives (韭菜) and plump shrimp seasoned with sesame oil, white pepper, and sometimes a touch of ginger, producing a fragrant, savory bite with a mild sweetness from the chives. Nutritionally, per 100 g they provide approximately 165 kcal, 6.8 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and 24.5 g carbohydrate, with 2.1 g dietary fibre and 320 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low), making them a moderate-carbohydrate, low-GI dim sum option. They are steamed, not fried, which keeps saturated fat low (~0.9 g/100 g) and avoids pro-inflammatory compounds from high-heat frying. Clinically, they are suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions (one 90 g serving, GL ≈ 11) and are a heart-friendly choice due to their low saturated fat and steaming method. Sodium is the primary concern for hypertensive individuals, particularly when paired with soy-based dipping sauces. Crystal Dumplings are traditionally served during yum cha (morning tea) and dim sum brunch, and are a staple of Cantonese cuisine originating from Guangdong Province, China.

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for crystal dumplings (wheat starch + tapioca starch skin with chive-shrimp filling). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central steamed shrimp-and-vegetable dumpling analogues and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore dim sum data. The AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with these references. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 for steamed wheat-starch dim sum items. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for crystal dumplings (wheat starch + tapioca starch skin, chive-shrimp filling, steamed) is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of April 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific steamed wheat-starch dumpling preparation is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of April 2026. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: steamed preparation with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per USDA analogous entries. 4. saturated_fat_g (0.9 g/100 g) and cholesterol_mg (55 mg/100 g) are estimated from shrimp and sesame oil fat profiles using USDA FoodData Central component data; no direct measurement available. 5. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on steamed wheat-starch dim sum items in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr); no direct GI measurement for crystal dumplings exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 90 g serving calculated as (50 × 22.1 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 11.0. 6. The input record listed two identical portion entries (90 g, is_default: true); deduplicated to one serving entry. 7. Halal status is marked false as shrimp-based dim sum preparation in traditional Cantonese kitchens may involve non-halal cross-contamination; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. Vitamin A value of 120 mcg RAE/100 g is plausible given garlic chive content and has been retained; unit interpreted as mcg RAE.
